In the [United States federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States"), **independent agencies** are [agencies](/wiki/List_of_federal_agencies_in_the_United_States "List of federal agencies in the United States") that exist outside the [federal executive departments](/wiki/United_States_federal_executive_departments "United States federal executive departments") (those headed by a [Cabinet](/wiki/United_States_Cabinet "United States Cabinet") secretary) and the [Executive Office of the President](/wiki/Executive_Office_of_the_President_of_the_United_States "Executive Office of the President of the United States").[[1]](#cite_note-Independent_Agencies_in_the_United_States-1): 6 In a narrower sense, the term refers only to those independent agencies that, while considered part of the [executive branch](/wiki/Executive_branch_of_the_United_States "Executive branch of the United States"), have [regulatory](/wiki/Administrative_law "Administrative law") or [rulemaking](/wiki/Rulemaking "Rulemaking") authority and are insulated from presidential control, usually because the president's power to dismiss the agency head or a member is limited.

Established through separate [statutes](/wiki/United_States_Code "United States Code") passed by [Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress"), each respective [statutory grant](/wiki/Organic_law "Organic law") of authority defines the goals the agency must work towards, as well as what substantive areas, if any, over which it may have the power of rulemaking. These agency rules (or regulations), when in force, have the power of federal law.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Executive and regulatory agencies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_agencies_of_the_United_States_federal_government&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Executive and regulatory agencies")]

Independent agencies exist outside the federal executive departments (those headed by a Cabinet secretary) and the Executive Office of the President.[[1]](#cite_note-Independent_Agencies_in_the_United_States-1): 6 There is a further distinction between independent executive agencies and independent *regulatory* agencies, which have been assigned rulemaking responsibilities or authorities by Congress. The [Paperwork Reduction Act](/wiki/Paperwork_Reduction_Act "Paperwork Reduction Act") lists 19 enumerated "independent regulatory agencies", such as the [Securities and Exchange Commission](/wiki/U.S._Securities_and_Exchange_Commission "U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission"), the [Federal Reserve](/wiki/Federal_Reserve "Federal Reserve"), the [Commodity Futures Trading Commission](/wiki/Commodity_Futures_Trading_Commission "Commodity Futures Trading Commission"), the [Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation](/wiki/Federal_Deposit_Insurance_Corporation "Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation"), and the [Consumer Financial Protection Bureau](/wiki/Consumer_Financial_Protection_Bureau "Consumer Financial Protection Bureau"). Generally, the heads of independent regulatory agencies can only be removed for cause, but Cabinet members and heads of independent executive agencies, such as the head of the [Environmental Protection Agency](/wiki/United_States_Environmental_Protection_Agency "United States Environmental Protection Agency"), serve "at the pleasure of the president" and can be removed without cause.[[3]](#cite_note-AdministrativeConference-3)

The degree to which the president has the power to use [executive orders](/wiki/Executive_order "Executive order") to set policy for independent executive agencies is disputed.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Many orders specifically exempt independent agencies, but some do not.[[5]](#cite_note-5) [Executive Order 12866](/wiki/Executive_Order_12866 "Executive Order 12866") has been a particular matter of controversy; it requires cost-benefit analysis for certain regulatory actions.[[3]](#cite_note-AdministrativeConference-3)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### Structure of regulatory agencies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_agencies_of_the_United_States_federal_government&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Structure of regulatory agencies")]

Independent agencies can be distinguished from the federal executive departments and other executive agencies by their structural and functional characteristics.[[8]](#cite_note-Pierce,_Richard_2009_p._101-8) Their officers can be protected from removal by the president, they can be controlled by a board that cannot be appointed all at once, and the board can be required to be bipartisan.

Presidential attempts to remove independent agency officials have generated most of the important [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") legal opinions in this area.[[9]](#cite_note-Pierce,_Shapiro_2009_p._102-9) In 1935, the Supreme Court in the case of *[Humphrey's Executor v. United States](/wiki/Humphrey%27s_Executor_v._United_States "Humphrey's Executor v. United States")* decided that although the president had the power to remove officials from agencies that were "an arm or an eye of the executive", it upheld statutory limitations on the president's power to remove officers of administrative bodies that performed quasi-legislative or quasi-judicial functions, such as the Federal Trade Commission.[[1]](#cite_note-Independent_Agencies_in_the_United_States-1): 142 Presidents normally do have the authority to remove regular executive agency heads [at will](/wiki/At-will_employment "At-will employment"), but they must meet the statutory requirements for removal of commissioners of independent agencies, such as demonstrating incapacity, [neglect of duty](/wiki/Neglect "Neglect"), [malfeasance](/wiki/Malfeasance "Malfeasance"), or other [good cause](/wiki/Good_cause "Good cause").[[10]](#cite_note-10) This precedent was weakened in 2025 when the Supreme Court issued an unsigned emergency stay[[11]](#cite_note-11) in the case of *Wilcox v. Trump*, reversing a temporary injunction issued by a district court (which was reversed by a three-judge panel but then affirmed by the full [DC Circuit Court](/wiki/DC_Circuit_Court "DC Circuit Court")). This allowed the removal of [Gwynne Wilcox](/wiki/Gwynne_Wilcox "Gwynne Wilcox"), head of the independent [National Labor Relations Board](/wiki/National_Labor_Relations_Board "National Labor Relations Board"), during the court case contesting the process and cause of her firing.

While most executive agencies have a single director, administrator, or secretary appointed by the [president of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States"), independent agencies (in the narrower sense of being outside presidential control) almost always have a commission, board, or similar [collegial body](/wiki/Collegiality "Collegiality") consisting of five to seven members who share power over the agency.[[8]](#cite_note-Pierce,_Richard_2009_p._101-8) (This is why many independent agencies include the word "Commission" or "Board" in their name.) The president appoints the [commissioners](/wiki/Commissioner "Commissioner") or [board members](/wiki/Board_member "Board member"), subject to [Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate") confirmation, but they often serve terms that are staggered and longer than a four-year presidential term,[[9]](#cite_note-Pierce,_Shapiro_2009_p._102-9) meaning that most presidents will not have the opportunity to appoint all the commissioners of a given independent agency. In addition, most independent agencies have a statutory requirement of [bipartisan](/wiki/Bipartisan "Bipartisan") membership on the commission, so the president cannot simply fill vacancies with members of his own political party.[[9]](#cite_note-Pierce,_Shapiro_2009_p._102-9) The president can normally designate which commissioner will serve as the chairperson.[[9]](#cite_note-Pierce,_Shapiro_2009_p._102-9)

Congress can designate certain agencies explicitly as "independent" in the governing statute, but the functional differences have more legal significance.[[12]](#cite_note-12) In reality, the high turnover rate among these commissioners or board members means that most presidents have the opportunity to fill enough vacancies to constitute a voting [majority](/wiki/Majority "Majority") on each independent agency commission within the first two years of the first term as president.[[13]](#cite_note-13) In some famous instances, presidents have found the independent agencies more loyal and in lockstep with the president's wishes and policy objectives than some dissenters among the [executive agency](/wiki/Executive_agency "Executive agency") [political appointments](/wiki/List_of_positions_filled_by_presidential_appointment_with_Senate_confirmation "List of positions filled by presidential appointment with Senate confirmation").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Although Congress can pass statutes limiting the circumstances under which the president can remove commissioners of independent agencies,[[15]](#cite_note-15) if the independent agency exercises any executive powers like enforcement, and most of them do, Congress cannot reserve removal power over executive officers to itself.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Constitutionally, Congress can only remove officers through [impeachment](/wiki/Federal_impeachment_in_the_United_States "Federal impeachment in the United States") proceedings. Members of Congress cannot serve as commissioners on independent agencies that have executive powers,[[17]](#cite_note-17) nor can Congress itself appoint the commissioners – the [Appointments Clause](/wiki/Appointments_Clause "Appointments Clause") of the Constitution vests that power in the president.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The Senate does participate, however, in appointments through "[advice and consent](/wiki/Advice_and_consent "Advice and consent")", which occurs through confirmation hearings and votes on the president's nominees.

## Examples of independent agencies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_agencies_of_the_United_States_federal_government&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Examples of independent agencies")]

These agencies are not represented in the cabinet and are not part of the Executive Office of the President:[[19]](#cite_note-19)

* The **[Central Intelligence Agency](/wiki/Central_Intelligence_Agency "Central Intelligence Agency") (CIA)** gathers foreign [intelligence](/wiki/Intelligence_assessment "Intelligence assessment") and provides [national security](/wiki/National_security_of_the_United_States "National security of the United States") assessments to policymakers in the United States. It acts as the primary human intelligence provider outside of the United States for the federal government. It is one of the principal members of the Intelligence Community, which is overseen by the **[Office of the Director of National Intelligence](/wiki/Office_of_the_Director_of_National_Intelligence "Office of the Director of National Intelligence") (ODNI)**, which is itself an independent agency.
* The **[Chemical Safety and Hazard Investigation Board](/wiki/U.S._Chemical_Safety_Board "U.S. Chemical Safety Board") (CSB)**, investigates industrial and chemical accidents and safety hazards.
* The **[Commodity Futures Trading Commission](/wiki/Commodity_Futures_Trading_Commission "Commodity Futures Trading Commission") (CFTC)** regulates [commodity](/wiki/Commodity "Commodity") [futures](/wiki/Futures_contract "Futures contract") and [option](/wiki/Option_(finance) "Option (finance)") markets in the United States. The agency protects market participants against manipulation, abusive trade practices, and fraud. Through oversight and regulation, the CFTC enables the markets to serve better their important functions in the US economy, providing a mechanism for [price discovery](/wiki/Price_discovery "Price discovery") and a means of offsetting price risk.
* The **[Consumer Financial Protection Bureau](/wiki/Consumer_Financial_Protection_Bureau "Consumer Financial Protection Bureau") (CFPB)** is responsible for consumer protection in the financial sector. Its jurisdiction includes banks, credit unions, securities firms, [payday lenders](/wiki/Payday_loans_in_the_United_States "Payday loans in the United States"), [mortgage-servicing operations](/wiki/Mortgage_servicer "Mortgage servicer"), foreclosure relief services, [debt collectors](/wiki/Debt_collection "Debt collection"), other financial companies in the United States.
* The **[Consumer Product Safety Commission](/wiki/U.S._Consumer_Product_Safety_Commission "U.S. Consumer Product Safety Commission") (CPSC)** evaluates and promotes the safety of consumer products.
* The **[Election Assistance Commission](/wiki/Election_Assistance_Commission "Election Assistance Commission") (EAC)** was formed in 2002 to serve as a national clearinghouse and resource of information regarding election administration. It is charged with administering payments to states and developing guidance to meet the [Help America Vote Act](/wiki/Help_America_Vote_Act "Help America Vote Act") (HAVA) requirements, adopting voluntary voting system guidelines, and accrediting voting system test laboratories and certifying voting equipment. It is also charged with developing and maintaining a national mail voter registration form.
* The **[Environmental Protection Agency](/wiki/United_States_Environmental_Protection_Agency "United States Environmental Protection Agency") (EPA)** works with state and local governments throughout the United States to control and abate [environmental pollution](/wiki/Pollution "Pollution") and to address problems related to solid waste, [pesticides](/wiki/Pesticide "Pesticide"), radiation, and toxic substances. The EPA sets and enforces standards for air, soil and water quality, evaluates the impact of pesticides and chemical substances, and manages the [Superfund](/wiki/Superfund "Superfund") program for cleaning toxic waste sites.
* The **[Export–Import Bank of the United States](/wiki/Export%E2%80%93Import_Bank_of_the_United_States "Export–Import Bank of the United States") (EXIM)** is the official export credit agency (ECA) of the United States federal government. Operating as a wholly owned federal government corporation, the bank "assists in financing and facilitating U.S. exports of goods and services", particularly when private sector lenders are unable or unwilling to provide financing.
* The **[Federal Communications Commission](/wiki/Federal_Communications_Commission "Federal Communications Commission") (FCC)** is charged with regulating interstate and international communications by radio, television, wire, satellite, and cable. It licenses radio and television broadcast stations, assigns radio frequencies, and enforces regulations designed to ensure that cable rates are reasonable. The FCC regulates common carriers, such as telephone and telegraph companies, as well as wireless telecommunications service providers.
* The **[Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation](/wiki/Federal_Deposit_Insurance_Corporation "Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation") (FDIC)** provides deposit insurance to depositors in U.S. commercial banks and savings banks. The FDIC was created by the 1933 Banking Act, enacted during the Great Depression to restore trust in the American banking system. Member banks' insurance dues are the primary source of funding.
* The **[Federal Election Commission](/wiki/Federal_Election_Commission "Federal Election Commission") (FEC)** oversees [campaign financing](/wiki/Campaign_finance_in_the_United_States "Campaign finance in the United States") for all federal elections. The commission oversees election rules as well as reporting of campaign contributions by the candidates.
* The **[Federal Energy Regulatory Commission](/wiki/Federal_Energy_Regulatory_Commission "Federal Energy Regulatory Commission") (FERC)** is the United States federal agency with jurisdiction over interstate electricity sales, wholesale electric rates, hydroelectric licensing, natural gas pricing, and oil pipeline rates. FERC also reviews and authorizes [liquefied natural gas](/wiki/Liquefied_natural_gas "Liquefied natural gas") (LNG) terminals, interstate natural gas pipelines, and non-federal [hydropower](/wiki/Hydropower "Hydropower") projects.
* The **[Federal Housing Finance Agency](/wiki/Federal_Housing_Finance_Agency "Federal Housing Finance Agency") (FHFA)** regulates [Fannie Mae](/wiki/Fannie_Mae "Fannie Mae"), [Freddie Mac](/wiki/Freddie_Mac "Freddie Mac"), and the 11 [Federal Home Loan Banks](/wiki/Federal_Home_Loan_Banks "Federal Home Loan Banks").
* The **[Federal Maritime Commission](/wiki/Federal_Maritime_Commission "Federal Maritime Commission") (FMC)** regulates the international ocean transportation of the United States. It is charged with ensuring a competitive and efficient ocean transportation system.[[20]](#cite_note-20)
* The [**Federal Mediation and Conciliation Service**](/wiki/Federal_Mediation_and_Conciliation_Service_(United_States) "Federal Mediation and Conciliation Service (United States)") **(FMCS)** created in 1947, is an independent agency whose mission is to preserve and promote labor-management peace and cooperation. Headquartered in Washington, DC, with six regions comprising more than 60 field and home offices, the agency provides mediation and conflict resolution services to industry, government agencies and communities.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Marriner_S._Eccles_Federal_Reserve_Board_Building.jpg/250px-Marriner_S._Eccles_Federal_Reserve_Board_Building.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marriner_S._Eccles_Federal_Reserve_Board_Building.jpg)

The headquarters of the Federal Reserve System

* The **[Federal Reserve System](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_System "Federal Reserve System")** (often called "the Fed"), is the [central bank](/wiki/Central_bank "Central bank") of the United States. It conducts the nation's [monetary policy](/wiki/Monetary_policy "Monetary policy") by influencing the volume of credit and money in circulation. The Federal Reserve regulates private banking institutions, works to contain [systemic risk](/wiki/Systemic_risk "Systemic risk") in financial markets, and provides certain financial services to the federal government, the public, and financial institutions.
* The **[Federal Retirement Thrift Investment Board](/wiki/Federal_Retirement_Thrift_Investment_Board "Federal Retirement Thrift Investment Board") (FRTIB)** is one of the smaller Executive Branch agencies, with just over 100 employees. It was established to administer the [Thrift Savings Plan](/wiki/Thrift_Savings_Plan "Thrift Savings Plan") (TSP), which provides federal employees the opportunity to save for additional retirement security. The Thrift Savings Plan is a tax-deferred defined contribution plan similar to a private sector [401(k)](/wiki/401(k) "401(k)") plan.
* The **[Federal Trade Commission](/wiki/Federal_Trade_Commission "Federal Trade Commission") (FTC)** enforces federal [antitrust](/wiki/United_States_antitrust_law "United States antitrust law") and [consumer protection](/wiki/Consumer_protection "Consumer protection") laws by investigating complaints against individual companies initiated by consumers, businesses, congressional inquiries, or reports in the media. The commission seeks to ensure that the nation's markets function competitively by eliminating unfair or deceptive practices.
* The **[General Services Administration](/wiki/General_Services_Administration "General Services Administration") (GSA)** is responsible for the purchase, supply, operation, and maintenance of federal property, buildings, and equipment, and for the sale of surplus items. GSA also manages the federal motor vehicle fleet and oversees [remote work](/wiki/Remote_work "Remote work") centers and civilian child care centers.
* The **[International Trade Commission](/wiki/United_States_International_Trade_Commission "United States International Trade Commission") (ITC)** provides trade expertise to both the legislative and executive branches of the federal government, determines the impact of imports on US industries, and directs actions against certain unfair trade practices, such as patent, trademark, and copyright infringement.
* The **[National Aeronautics and Space Administration](/wiki/NASA "NASA") (NASA)** is the federal government's space agency. It is responsible for the civilian space program as well as aeronautics and aerospace research.
* The **[National Archives and Records Administration](/wiki/National_Archives_and_Records_Administration "National Archives and Records Administration") (NARA)** preserves the nation's history by overseeing the management of all federal records. The holdings of the National Archives include original textual materials, motion picture films, sound and video recordings, maps, still pictures, and computer data. The Declaration of Independence, the US Constitution, and the Bill of Rights are preserved and displayed at the National Archives building in Washington, D.C.
* The **[National Credit Union Administration](/wiki/National_Credit_Union_Administration "National Credit Union Administration") (NCUA)**, government backer of [credit unions](/wiki/Credit_unions_in_the_United_States "Credit unions in the United States"), equivalent to the [FDIC](/wiki/Federal_Deposit_Insurance_Corporation "Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation").
* The **[National Labor Relations Board](/wiki/National_Labor_Relations_Board "National Labor Relations Board") (NLRB)** administers the principal [United States labor law](/wiki/United_States_labor_law "United States labor law"), the [National Labor Relations Act](/wiki/National_Labor_Relations_Act "National Labor Relations Act"). The board is vested with the power to prevent or remedy unfair labor practices and to safeguard employees' rights to organize and determine through elections whether to have a union as their bargaining representative.
* The **[National Science Foundation](/wiki/National_Science_Foundation "National Science Foundation") (NSF)** supports fundamental research and education in all the non-medical fields of science and engineering.
* The **[National Transportation Safety Board](/wiki/National_Transportation_Safety_Board "National Transportation Safety Board") (NTSB)** is responsible for civil transportation [accident analysis](/wiki/Accident_analysis "Accident analysis") in the US.[[21]](#cite_note-21) The NTSB investigates and reports on [aviation accidents and incidents](/wiki/Aviation_accidents_and_incidents "Aviation accidents and incidents"), certain types of [car accidents](/wiki/Car_accident "Car accident"), [ship and marine accidents](/wiki/Ship_transport "Ship transport"), [pipeline transport](/wiki/Pipeline_transport "Pipeline transport") accidents, and [rail transport](/wiki/Rail_transport "Rail transport") accidents.[[22]](#cite_note-49_USC_1131-22)
* The **[Nuclear Regulatory Commission](/wiki/Nuclear_Regulatory_Commission "Nuclear Regulatory Commission") (NRC)** was established by the [Energy Reorganization Act of 1974](/wiki/Energy_Reorganization_Act_of_1974 "Energy Reorganization Act of 1974") from the [United States Atomic Energy Commission](/wiki/United_States_Atomic_Energy_Commission "United States Atomic Energy Commission"), and opened January 19, 1975. The NRC oversees reactor safety and security, reactor licensing and renewal, radioactive material safety, and spent fuel management (storage, security, recycling, and disposal). In 2025, President [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") effectively ended its independence through a series of executive orders directing specific reforms.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)
* The **[Office of Special Counsel](/wiki/United_States_Office_of_Special_Counsel "United States Office of Special Counsel") (OSC)** is a permanent investigative and prosecutorial agency that operates a secure channel for federal whistleblower disclosures, protects federal employees from reprisal for whistleblowing, and enforces the restrictions of the Hatch Act on partisan political activity by government employees.
* The **[Peace Corps](/wiki/Peace_Corps "Peace Corps")** was created in 1961 by an executive order of President Kennedy, originally under the State Department but reorganized as an independent agency by President Nixon. Peace Corps' goal is to assist developing countries by providing skilled workers in fields such as education, health, entrepreneurship, women's empowerment, or community development.
* The **[Postal Regulatory Commission](/wiki/Postal_Regulatory_Commission "Postal Regulatory Commission") (PRC)** was created in 1971 as the Postal Rate Commission and strengthened under the [Postal Accountability and Enhancement Act](/wiki/Postal_Accountability_and_Enhancement_Act "Postal Accountability and Enhancement Act") enacted in December 2006. Provides regulatory oversight over the activities of the [United States Postal Service](/wiki/United_States_Postal_Service "United States Postal Service").
* The **[Securities and Exchange Commission](/wiki/U.S._Securities_and_Exchange_Commission "U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission") (SEC)** was established to protect investors who buy stocks and bonds. Federal laws require companies that plan to raise money by selling their own securities to file reports about their operations with the SEC, so that investors have access to all material information. The commission has powers to prevent or punish fraud in the sale of securities and is authorized to regulate [stock exchanges](/wiki/Stock_exchange "Stock exchange").
* The **[Selective Service System](/wiki/Selective_Service_System "Selective Service System") (SSS)** is an independent federal agency operating with permanent authorization under the [Military Selective Service Act](/wiki/Military_Selective_Service_Act "Military Selective Service Act"). It is not part of the [Department of Defense](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Defense "United States Department of Defense"); however, it exists to serve the emergency manpower needs of the military by conscripting untrained men, or personnel with professional health care skills, if directed by Congress and the president. Its statutory missions also include being ready to administer an alternative service program, in lieu of military service for men classified as [conscientious objectors](/wiki/Conscientious_objector "Conscientious objector").
* The **[Small Business Administration](/wiki/Small_Business_Administration "Small Business Administration") (SBA)** was created in 1953 to advise, assist, and protect the interests of small business concerns. The SBA guarantees loans to small businesses, aids victims of floods and other natural disasters, promotes the growth of minority-owned firms, and helps secure contracts for small businesses to supply goods and services to the federal government.
* The **[Social Security Administration](/wiki/Social_Security_Administration "Social Security Administration") (SSA)** is the United States federal agency that administers [Social Security](/wiki/Social_Security_(United_States) "Social Security (United States)"), a [social insurance](/wiki/Social_insurance "Social insurance") program consisting of retirement, disability, and survivors' benefits. To qualify for these benefits, most American workers pay Social Security taxes on their earnings; future benefits are based on employee contributions.
* The **[Surface Transportation Board](/wiki/Surface_Transportation_Board "Surface Transportation Board") (STB)** was created in the ICC Termination Act of 1995 and is the successor agency to the [Interstate Commerce Commission](/wiki/Interstate_Commerce_Commission "Interstate Commerce Commission"). The STB is an economic regulatory agency that Congress charged with resolving railroad rate and service disputes and reviewing proposed railroad mergers. The STB is decisionally independent, although it is administratively affiliated with the Department of Transportation.
* The **[Tennessee Valley Authority](/wiki/Tennessee_Valley_Authority "Tennessee Valley Authority") (TVA)**, created in 1933, provides economic development to the Tennessee Valley, a region particularly affected by the Great Depression.
* The **[United States Agency for International Development](/wiki/United_States_Agency_for_International_Development "United States Agency for International Development") (USAID)**, created in 1961 to provide foreign aid and assist with international development. In 2025, President Trump's administration terminated most of the agency’s programs[[25]](#cite_note-25) and rolled the remaining programs into the State Department,[[26]](#cite_note-:14-26) but only an act of Congress can formally abolish the agency.[[27]](#cite_note-27)
* The **[United States Office of Personnel Management](/wiki/United_States_Office_of_Personnel_Management "United States Office of Personnel Management") (OPM)** manages the United States federal civil service by providing federal human resources policy, oversight, and support, and tends to healthcare (FEHB), life insurance (FEGLI), and retirement benefits (CSRS and FERS, but not TSP) for federal government employees, retirees, and their dependents. It is headed by a director, who is nominated by the President.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/USPS_Truck_3137.jpg/250px-USPS_Truck_3137.jpg)](/wiki/File:USPS_Truck_3137.jpg)

A USPS truck in the snow

* The **[United States Postal Service](/wiki/United_States_Postal_Service "United States Postal Service") (USPS)** is defined by statute as an "independent establishment" of the federal government, which replaced the Cabinet-level [Post Office Department](/wiki/Post_Office_Department "Post Office Department") in 1971. The Postal Service is responsible for the collection, transportation, and delivery of the mails, and for the operation of thousands of local post offices across the country. It also provides international mail service through the [Universal Postal Union](/wiki/Universal_Postal_Union "Universal Postal Union") and other agreements with foreign countries.
* The **[United States Trade and Development Agency](/wiki/United_States_Trade_and_Development_Agency "United States Trade and Development Agency") (USTDA)**, which advances economic development and U.S. commercial interests in developing and middle income countries.

### Former agencies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_agencies_of_the_United_States_federal_government&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Former agencies")]

* The **[Committee on Public Information](/wiki/Committee_on_Public_Information "Committee on Public Information")** was an agency created to influence US public opinion regarding American participation in [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"). Lasting from April 14, 1917, to June 30, 1919 it was directed by [George Creel](/wiki/George_Creel "George Creel"). The agency used propaganda available to achieve its goals.
* The **[Interstate Commerce Commission](/wiki/Interstate_Commerce_Commission "Interstate Commerce Commission")** regulated [common carriers](/wiki/Common_carrier "Common carrier") and was thus able to render far reaching orders, such as the desegregation of public transportation. After trucking and railroads were largely deregulated, the ICC was replaced with the independent [Surface Transportation Board](/wiki/Surface_Transportation_Board "Surface Transportation Board"), with remaining functions transferred to the Department of Transportation.
* The **[United States Maritime Commission](/wiki/United_States_Maritime_Commission "United States Maritime Commission")** was intended to formulate a merchant shipbuilding program to design and build five hundred modern merchant cargo ships for the [US Merchant Marine](/wiki/United_States_Merchant_Marine "United States Merchant Marine"). It also formed the [United States Maritime Service](/wiki/United_States_Maritime_Service "United States Maritime Service"). In 1950, its functions were transferred to the [United States Maritime Administration](/wiki/United_States_Maritime_Administration "United States Maritime Administration"), which later became part of the [Department of Transportation](/wiki/US_Department_of_Transportation "US Department of Transportation").
* The **[Reconstruction Finance Corporation](/wiki/Reconstruction_Finance_Corporation "Reconstruction Finance Corporation")** was designed to help finance projects during the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"). It later helped finance the nation's military buildup as [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") approached. Scandals led to its eventual dissolution in 1956.
* The **[Atomic Energy Commission](/wiki/United_States_Atomic_Energy_Commission "United States Atomic Energy Commission")** was established for peacetime development of atomic energy. In 1974 it was abolished, and two new agencies were created; the **[Nuclear Regulatory Commission](/wiki/Nuclear_Regulatory_Commission "Nuclear Regulatory Commission")** (NRC) for regulation of civilian uses of nuclear materials, such as nuclear power plants and medical uses, and the **[Energy Research and Development Administration](/wiki/Energy_Research_and_Development_Administration "Energy Research and Development Administration")** (ERDA) for military development of nuclear materials.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] In 1977, the ERDA was merged with the [Federal Energy Administration](/wiki/Federal_Energy_Administration "Federal Energy Administration") to form the [United States Department of Energy](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Energy "United States Department of Energy").[[28]](#cite_note-28)
* The **[Office of the United States Nuclear Waste Negotiator](/wiki/Office_of_the_United_States_Nuclear_Waste_Negotiator "Office of the United States Nuclear Waste Negotiator")** was responsible for the placement and long term storage of [radioactive waste](/wiki/Radioactive_waste "Radioactive waste"). It was dissolved in 1995.[[29]](#cite_note-29)
* The **[United States Information Agency](/wiki/United_States_Information_Agency "United States Information Agency")**, which was partially merged into the [Department of State](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_State "United States Department of State") in 1999.

## Agencies outside of executive branch

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_agencies_of_the_United_States_federal_government&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Agencies outside of executive branch")]

Although not officially part of the executive branch, these agencies are required by federal statute to release certain information about their programs and activities into the [Federal Register](/wiki/Federal_Register "Federal Register"), the daily journal of government activities:

* **[Fannie Mae](/wiki/Fannie_Mae "Fannie Mae"), or the Federal National Mortgage Association (FNMA)**
* **[Freddie Mac](/wiki/Freddie_Mac "Freddie Mac"), or the Federal Home Loan Mortgage Corporation (FHLMC)**
* The **[National Gallery of Art](/wiki/National_Gallery_of_Art "National Gallery of Art")**
* The **[Smithsonian Institution](/wiki/Smithsonian_Institution "Smithsonian Institution") (SI)** is an independent establishment of the United States created by an act of Congress on August 10, 1846. The SI conducts scientific and scholarly research; publishes the results of studies, explorations, and investigations; organizes exhibits representative of the arts, the sciences, American history, and world culture; and engages in educational programming and national and international cooperative research. Most of the National Museums in Washington, D.C., are part of the Smithsonian.

## Proposed independent agencies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_agencies_of_the_United_States_federal_government&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Proposed independent agencies")]

* Federal Elections Agency, proposed by [Lee Drutman](/wiki/Lee_Drutman "Lee Drutman") of [New America](/wiki/New_America_(organization) "New America (organization)"),[[30]](#cite_note-30) which would merge the roles of the [Federal Election Commission](/wiki/Federal_Election_Commission "Federal Election Commission") and [Election Assistance Commission](/wiki/Election_Assistance_Commission "Election Assistance Commission"), expand enforcement against election interference and gather election data
* Weather Modification Operations and Research Board, proposed by [Kay Bailey Hutchison](/wiki/Kay_Bailey_Hutchison "Kay Bailey Hutchison") to promote research into [weather modification](/wiki/Weather_modification "Weather modification")
* Nuclear Waste Administration,[[31]](#cite_note-31) proposed by [Lisa Murkowski](/wiki/Lisa_Murkowski "Lisa Murkowski") to provide for the permanent disposal of spent nuclear fuel and high-level radioactive waste.
